subject: otolaryngology
title: Epistaxis
preambleMarkdown:
# Epistaxis

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Epistaxis is a clinical condition or syndrome in otolaryngology.
- This article focuses on ear, nose, throat, airway, hearing, vestibular, sinus, and head-neck pathology.
- The practical clinical priority is otolaryngology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Epistaxis to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Epistaxis is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is otolaryngology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Epistaxis depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In otolaryngology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Epistaxis.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Epistaxis disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Epistaxis should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Epistaxis first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Epistaxis, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Epistaxis, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused otolaryngology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Epistaxis presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Epistaxis with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Epistaxis is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Epistaxis should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Epistaxis is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses otolaryngology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Epistaxis, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Epistaxis may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Epistaxis patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Epistaxis, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Epistaxis in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Epistaxis, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Epistaxis does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Epistaxis may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Epistaxis, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Epistaxis as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Epistaxis draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

### Source Excerpt: Nose

_Source lines: 23508-23884._

```text
III. NOSE
A. Sinusitis

## 1. Discussion

a. Definition. Sinusitis is an inflammation of the paranasal (ethmoid, frontal,
maxillary, or sphenoid) sinuses. It is classified as acute or chronic, depend-
ing on how long the infection has been present.

b. Etiology and pathogenesis. Sinusitis occurs when pus is trapped in the sinus
and is unable to drain completely through the ostia into the nasal cavity.

i. Bacterial causes include H. influenzae, S. pneumoniae, streptococci, and
M. catarrhalis.

ii. Fungal causes are most common in patients with diabetes. Mucormyco-
sis is particularly life-threatening.

c. Predisposing factors include chronic nasal edema, nasal polyps, nasal aller-
gies, preceding upper respiratory tract infection, and upper tooth abscess.

2. Clinical features
a. Symptoms
i. Pain. Nasal congestion increases the pressure in the sinus, leading
to pain that often worsens when the patient bends over. Patients may
complain of a headache . Facial pain often corresponds to the affected
sinus:
a) Cheeks and upper teeth-maxillary sinus
b) Eyebrow area-frontal sinus
c) Eyes or behind the eyes-ethmoid sinus
ii. Generalized malaise and periorbital edema are common complaints.

iii. Nasal discharge is often blood-tinged and purulent. A postnasal drip
may cause coughing and a sore throat.

b. Physical examination findings
i. Fever is often present in acute sinusitis but is rare in chronic sinusitis.

ii. Tenderness to percussion may be present over the affected sinus.

iii. Opacity of the affected sinus may be evident on transillumination .

A negative transillumination does not rule out sinusitis because the test
is only positive when the sinusitis is advanced.

3. Differential diagnoses include viral rhinitis, allergic rhinitis, vasomotor rhini-
tis, tumors, foreign bodies, and Wegener granulomatosis.

## 4. Evaluation

a. Laboratory studies. Typically not indicated. In acute sinusitis, a CBC may
reveal an elevated white blood cell count.

b. Imaging studies
i. Radiographs. Plain sinus films may reveal air-fluid levels, cloudiness, or
thickening of the sinus mucosa.

ii. CT scans may identify an underlying predisposing anatomic condition
(e.g., abnormally narrowed outlets, tumorous or cystic obstructions).

## 5. Therapy

a. Supportive measures
i. An effort should be made to avoid air-borne irritants (e.g., smoke,
pollen).

292 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Eye, Ear, Nose, Throat, and Dental Emergencies
ii. Although steam inhalation and environmental humidification are useful
adjuncts, aggressive systemic rehydration is essential to mobilize and
prevent reaccumulation of the thickened secretions.

iii. Analgesics, decongestants, antihistamines, and steroids may also be
helpful.

b. Antibiotic therapy. Not indicated in most cases. If determined necessary,
acceptable regimens include:
i. Amoxicillin clavulanate (500 mg three times daily for 2 to 3 weeks)
ii. Doxycycline (100 mg twice daily for 10 days)
c. Surgery. Surgical drainage of the sinus may be indicated if medical treatment
fails. Surgery may also be indicated to correct underlying anatomic defects.

6. Disposition. All patients diagnosed with sinusitis should be followed up until
clinically cured.

a. Discharge. In the absence of complications (e.g., orbital cellulitis, osteo-
myelitis, septic cavernous thrombosis, contiguous spread to involve the
nervous system), patients can be sent home with instructions to follow up
in 1 to 2 days.

b. Admission is indicated for extremely ill patients, patients with orbital
edema, and patients with nuchal rigidity or altered mental status.

B. Foreign body
1. Discussion. Nasal foreign bodies are seen most often in children.

2. Clinical features
a. Patient history. Sometimes, a parent brings the child to the ED after witness-
ing the child or the child's sibling place an object (e.g., a bead, bean, sponge,
toy) in the child's nose. However, more often the parent brings the child to the
ED after noticing a foul-smelling, purulent discharge from the child's nose.

b. Symptoms. The patient may be asymptomatic, or foul, purulent rhinorrhea
or epistaxis may be present.

c. Physical examination findings. Often, the foreign body can be identified
using a nasal speculum and a headlamp. It may be necessary to suction the
nares to clean away purulent secretions in order to visualize the foreign body.

3. Differential diagnoses include sinusitis and rhinitis.

4. Evaluation. Physical examination is usually adequate for diagnosis. Fiberoptic
evaluation may be necessary if the object is in the posterior or turbinate areas.

## 5. Therapy

a. Object removal. Pediatric patients need to be restrained in a papoose or
rolled in a sheet to minimize movement during examination and removal.

Some children may require sedation.

i. Forceful expulsion. One method that often works is to have the parent seal
off the opposite naris and blow into the child's mouth in a manner similar
to that used during mouth-to-mouth resuscitation. The result is the expul-
sion of the object and a fair amount of mucus through the other naris.

ii. A suction catheter with the tip cut off flat can be introduced into the
nasal passage until the tip contacts the object, at which point suction is
applied.

iii. Alligator forceps can be used to grab the object. With beads, it is best
to rotate the bead so that the hole can be grasped with the forceps.

b. Supportive care. After removal of the object, saline drops can be instilled to
moisten the nasal passage. If pus is present, a course of antibiotics should
be prescribed to treat possible purulent rhinitis or sinusitis.

6. Disposition. If attempts at removing the object in the ED are successful, the
patient can be sent home. If the object cannot be removed in the ED, an otolar-
yngologist should be consulted because the patient may need admission to the
hospital for surgical removal of the object.

C. Epistaxis

## 1. Discussion

a. Etiology. There are many causes of nosebleeds; most can be classified as be-
longing to one of the following three groups.

E Y E , E A R , N O S E , T H R O A T , A N D D E N T A L E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	293

Eye, Ear, Nose, Throat, and Dental Emergencies
i. Local causes include trauma (e.g., nose picking, foreign bodies, nasal
fractures), inflammation, upper respiratory tract infection, and environ-
mental irritants.

ii. Regional causes include vascular abnormalities, neoplasms, and ectopic
endometriosis.

iii. Systemic causes include intrinsic and drug-induced coagulopathies,
thrombocytopenia, leukemia, hepatic disease, and infections. Hyperten-
sion is not in itself a cause of epistaxis because the relative frequency in
patients with hypertension is the same as in the general population.

b. Location. It is important to differentiate anterior bleeds from posterior
bleeds because anterior bleeds are generally easy to treat, whereas posterior
bleeds are more difficult.

i. Anterior bleeds usually occur in Kiesselbach plexus on the anterior
nasal septum and account for 90% of all nose bleeds. They may also
occur on the anterior inferior turbinate.

ii. Posterior bleeds occur from the sphenopalatine artery or in the naso-
pharynx, often from branches of the carotid arteries.

2. Clinical findings
a. Anterior bleeds are almost always unilateral, unless the nasal septum is per-
forated. Asking the patient to pinch his nose (thereby applying pressure to
Kiesselbach plexus) will tamponade most anterior bleeds, whereas posterior
bleeds will continue to bleed unabated.

b. Posterior bleeds. Because posterior bleeds occur behind the septum, the
blood will often exit both nares and large clots can accumulate in the
nasopharynx.

3. Evaluation. Most patients with epistaxis do not require any laboratory test-
ing. However, additional studies may be indicated in patients with a history of
bleeding problems, oral anticoagulant use, possible hepatic disease, or bleeding
that is difficult to control.

a. A CBC to check hemoglobin and look for thrombocytopenia or leukemia
b. A prothrombin time and partial thromboplastin time to look for
overanticoagulation or hepatic dysfunction
c. A blood type and cross-match in case transfusion is necessary to compen-
sate for ongoing blood loss

## 4. Therapy

a. Initial stabilization. In a patient who presents with hypotension and tachy-
cardia, an intravenous line should be started, blood samples should be
drawn, and fluid resuscitation should be initiated immediately. An adequate
airway should be ensured in all patients.

b. Terminating bleeding
i. Preliminary preparations. Hospital staff should be gloved, gowned, and
wearing protective eyewear. The following equipment should be readied:
a) A bright light source
b) Suction (turned on and connected)
c) A nasal speculum, Bayonet forceps, 4" # 4" gauze pads, cotton
swabs, cotton balls, a medication cup, silver nitrate sticks, Surgicel
(Ethicon, Somerville, NJ) or a nasal tampon, petroleum jelly gauze
strips, and two no. 10 Foley catheters
d) Cocaine 4% solution or viscous solution, lidocaine 1% solution, and
phenylephrine 1% solution
ii. Clearing the nasal passages. The patient should be seated in a protec-
tive gown with a basin to collect blood. While waiting for treatment,
the patient should pinch his or her nose to attempt to slow the bleed-
ing. Once the necessary equipment is assembled, the patient should be
asked to gently blow his or her nose and clear his or her throat to get
as much blood and clot out of the nasal cavity as possible.

iii. Anesthesia. Cocaine-soaked pledgets or cotton swabs are placed
in the nares to anesthetize the mucous membranes and cause
Quick 	HIT
Pulse oximetry and a cardiac
monitor should be used in
elderly and complicated
epistaxis patients.

294 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Eye, Ear, Nose, Throat, and Dental Emergencies
vasoconstriction. (These effects usually occur after 5 to 10 minutes.)

Alternatively, a mixture of lidocaine and phenylephrine can be used.

iv. Inspection. When adequate anesthesia has been obtained, a headlamp
with a bright light source is used to insert the nasal speculum in the
nares. The anterior septum should be inspected for the source of the
bleeding (a clot may be seen if the bleeding has stopped). Suctioning
may be necessary to visualize the source. The absence of an anterior
bleed and continued epistaxis point to a more serious posterior bleed.

a) If the bleeding has stopped, the patient should be observed for 15 to
30 minutes. No additional treatment is necessary.

b) If the bleeding has not stopped, the evaluation must continue.

v. Tamponade and cautery. When the source of the bleeding is found, an
attempt to tamponade the bleeding with an applicator or phenylephrine
should be made. If an anterior bleed cannot be stopped by applying
pressure, cautery can be attempted with the silver nitrate stick.

vi. Anterior packing. If both tamponade and cautery fail, it will prob-
ably be necessary to pack the anterior nose. Often both nares need to
be packed to get adequate tamponade with nasal tampons, Vaseline
(Unilever, Rotterdam, Netherlands) gauze, or Gelfoam (Pfizer,
New York, NY).

vii. Posterior packing is necessary for posterior bleeds.

a) There are two common methods:
1) Commercially available posterior packing balloons can be in-
serted in both nares and inflated as per the insert instructions.

2) The alternative is posterior gauze packing (Figure 12-7).

b) Failure to control the posterior bleeding may require operative
management or angiography and embolization.

## 5. Disposition

a. Discharge
i. Bleeds controlled by pressure tamponade. Patients should be observed
for 15 to 30 minutes to ensure that rebleeding does not occur. The pa-
tient can be discharged with "nosebleed instructions" (i.e., no picking or
blowing the nose, no sneezing through the nose, no bending forward or
lifting). Patients should be advised to use a humidifier and to coat the
nares with petroleum jelly the next day.

ii. Bleeds controlled by anterior packs. Patients should be observed for 15 to
30 minutes to ensure tamponade. An antibiotic (e.g., amoxicillin) should be
prescribed to prevent sinusitis and the patient should have an appointment
with an otolaryngologist 2 to 3 days later to have the packing removed.

b. Admission
i. Patients with multiple recent bleeds, severe anemia, debilitating
illnesses, or hepatic dysfunction, or those who are taking antico-
agulants and are supratherapeutic probably need to be admitted for
observation.

ii. Patients with posterior bleeds require hospital admission, usually
in a monitored setting (i.e., the intensive care unit or a step-down
unit). These patients are at risk of rebleeding, airway obstruction, and
profound hypoxemia. Prophylactic antibiotics should be administered to
prevent sinusitis and toxic shock syndrome.

D. Nasal fractures
1. Discussion. Nasal fractures are the most common fractures of the face and
are often misdiagnosed. Nasal fractures are often associated with epistaxis and
septal hematomas.

2. Clinical features include pain in the nasal bridge with swelling, ecchymosis, and de-
formity. A septal hematoma should be ruled out by directly visualizing the septum.

3. Differential diagnoses include more severe fractures involving the cribriform
plate, ethmoidal bones, or lacrimal bones. More severe fractures may be
associated with the leakage of cerebrospinal fluid into the nasal cavity.

E Y E , E A R , N O S E , T H R O A T , A N D D E N T A L E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	295

Eye, Ear, Nose, Throat, and Dental Emergencies
4. Evaluation. Usually, the clinical findings of pain, crepitance, and deformity are
adequate for diagnosis. Radiographs can be used if uncertainty regarding the
diagnosis remains.

## 5. Therapy

a. Repair
i. Minimally displaced fractures are usually treated pain control alone.

ii. More severely displaced fractures are treated with immediate or de-
layed reduction.

a) Immediate reduction is recommended if the patient is hemorrhaging
or if there is marked deformity.

b) Delayed reduction. Often, delayed reduction is preferable because it
is performed after the swelling has subsided, facilitating evaluation
of outcome.

b. Supportive care. Epistaxis must be controlled, and septal hematomas re-
quire evacuation. A patient requiring packing should be given prophylactic
antibodies to prevent sinusitis.

## 6. Disposition

a. Discharge. Most patients with nasal fractures can be discharged with
instructions to follow-up with an otolaryngologist.

F I G U R E

staxis. (A) Use of gauze packing
in posterior nasopharynx with
anterior anchoring to prevent mi-
gration of the gauze. Note vase-

of bleeding. (B) Use of Foley
catheter packing in posterior na-
sopharynx with anterior anchor-
ing to prevent migration of the
bulb. An umbilical clip attached
to the end of the catheter can
alternately be used externally to
prevent migration.

A
B
296 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Eye, Ear, Nose, Throat, and Dental Emergencies
b. Admission. Indications for admission include:
i. Failure to control hemorrhage
ii. The presence of other injuries requiring hospitalization
iii. The presence of a cerebrospinal fluid leak associated with deeper
fractures in the nasal cavity, the cribriform plate, or the ethmoidal bones
```

<!-- source: step-up-emergency-medicine import end -->

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.
